who wrote genesis: Moses is the traditional author of Genesis (and the Pentateuch), compiled under divine inspiration. Jesus and the apostles attribute the Law to Moses (Luke 24:27, John 5:46-47). Genesis covers creation through the death of Joseph (~1800 BC).
who wrote exodus: Moses wrote Exodus, the second book of the Pentateuch. It records Israel's deliverance from Egypt, the giving of the Law at Sinai, and the construction of the tabernacle (~1446 BC).
who wrote leviticus: Moses wrote Leviticus. It contains the priestly laws, sacrificial system, and holiness code given at Sinai. Key verse: 'Be holy, for I am holy' (Lev 19:2).
who wrote numbers: Moses wrote Numbers. It records Israel's 40 years of wilderness wandering, the census, and the failure of the first generation to enter the promised land.
who wrote deuteronomy: Moses wrote Deuteronomy (~1406 BC), his final addresses to Israel before entering Canaan. Joshua likely completed the account of Moses' death (Deut 34).
who wrote joshua: Joshua is the primary author, with later editorial additions (possibly by Samuel or a later scribe). It records the conquest and division of Canaan.
who wrote judges: Tradition attributes Judges to Samuel. It covers the period between Joshua and the monarchy (~1375-1050 BC), showing Israel's cycle of sin, oppression, repentance, and deliverance.
who wrote ruth: Author unknown; Jewish tradition attributes it to Samuel. Set during the judges period, it tells of Ruth's loyalty and redemption through Boaz, ancestor of David and Christ.
who wrote 1 samuel: Author uncertain; tradition attributes it to Samuel (with Nathan and Gad completing it after his death). It covers Samuel's ministry, Saul's rise and fall, and David's anointing.
who wrote 2 samuel: Same as 1 Samuel — likely Samuel, Nathan, and Gad. It records David's reign over all Israel, his successes and failures.
who wrote 1 kings: Jewish tradition attributes Kings to Jeremiah. It covers Solomon's reign through the division of the kingdom to Jehoiachin's release (~560 BC).
who wrote 2 kings: Same as 1 Kings — traditionally Jeremiah. It continues the history of the divided kingdom through the fall of Israel (722 BC) and Judah (586 BC).
who wrote ezra: Ezra wrote Ezra (and likely Chronicles). A priest and scribe, he led the second return from Babylon and the religious reformation (~458 BC).
who wrote nehemiah: Nehemiah wrote Nehemiah, his memoir of rebuilding Jerusalem's walls and reforms as governor (~445-425 BC).
who wrote esther: Author unknown; possibly Mordecai or Ezra. It records God's providential deliverance of the Jews through Esther during the Persian exile.
who wrote job: Author unknown; possibly Job himself, Moses, or Solomon. Set in the patriarchal period, it explores suffering, sovereignty, and faith.
who wrote psalms: Multiple authors: David (73 psalms), Asaph (12), the sons of Korah (11), Solomon (2), Moses (1), Ethan (1), and anonymous. Compiled over ~1000 years.
who wrote proverbs: Solomon wrote most of Proverbs (chs 1-24, 25-29). Agur wrote ch 30, and King Lemuel's mother ch 31. Compiled by Hezekiah's men (Prov 25:1).
who wrote ecclesiastes: Solomon wrote Ecclesiastes ('the Preacher,' son of David, king in Jerusalem). It reflects on the vanity of life apart from God.
who wrote song of solomon: Solomon wrote the Song of Solomon (Song 1:1). It is a poetic celebration of marital love, often read allegorically as Christ and the Church.
who wrote isaiah: Isaiah wrote Isaiah (~740-680 BC), prophesying to Judah during the reigns of Uzziah through Hezekiah. He is called the 'evangelical prophet' for his messianic prophecies (Isa 53).
who wrote jeremiah: Jeremiah wrote Jeremiah (dictated to Baruch, Jer 36:4). He prophesied to Judah before and during the Babylonian exile (~627-580 BC), the 'weeping prophet.'
who wrote lamentations: Jeremiah wrote Lamentations, mourning the destruction of Jerusalem in 586 BC. Yet it declares: 'The steadfast love of the Lord never ceases' (Lam 3:22).
who wrote ezekiel: Ezekiel wrote Ezekiel (~593-571 BC), prophesying to the exiles in Babylon. Key visions: the glory of the Lord, the valley of dry bones, the new temple.
who wrote daniel: Daniel wrote Daniel (~605-535 BC). Taken to Babylon as a youth, he served under four empires. Key themes: God's sovereignty over kingdoms, the Son of Man, the 70 weeks.
who wrote hosea: Hosea wrote Hosea (~750-715 BC), prophesying to the northern kingdom. His marriage to Gomer symbolized God's faithfulness to unfaithful Israel.
who wrote joel: Joel wrote Joel (date debated, possibly ~835 BC or post-exilic). Key themes: the day of the Lord, the locust plague, the outpouring of the Spirit (Joel 2:28, cited at Pentecost).
who wrote amos: Amos wrote Amos (~760 BC), a shepherd from Tekoa called to prophesy to the northern kingdom. Theme: justice and righteousness (Amos 5:24).
who wrote obadiah: Obadiah wrote Obadiah (shortest OT book, 21 verses). It pronounces judgment on Edom for their violence against Judah.
who wrote jonah: Jonah wrote Jonah (~780 BC). He prophesied to Nineveh; the book shows God's mercy to Gentiles and His sovereignty over creation.
who wrote micah: Micah wrote Micah (~735-700 BC), a contemporary of Isaiah. Key prophecy: Bethlehem as Messiah's birthplace (Mic 5:2). Theme: do justice, love mercy, walk humbly (Mic 6:8).
who wrote nahum: Nahum wrote Nahum (~650 BC), prophesying the fall of Nineveh (~100 years after Jonah's revival).
who wrote habakkuk: Habakkuk wrote Habakkuk (~620 BC). He questions God's justice and receives the foundational verse: 'The just shall live by his faith' (Hab 2:4, cited in Rom 1:17, Gal 3:11, Heb 10:38).
who wrote zephaniah: Zephaniah wrote Zephaniah (~640 BC), during Josiah's reforms. Theme: the day of the Lord and a remnant preserved.
who wrote haggai: Haggai wrote Haggai (~520 BC), urging the returned exiles to rebuild the temple. Theme: put God's house first.
who wrote zechariah: Zechariah wrote Zechariah (~520-480 BC), a contemporary of Haggai. Key messianic prophecies: the King on a donkey (Zech 9:9), the pierced one (Zech 12:10), the Shepherd struck (Zech 13:7).
who wrote malachi: Malachi wrote Malachi (~450 BC), the last OT prophet. He rebukes priestly corruption and foretells Elijah's coming (Mal 4:5, fulfilled in John the Baptist).
who wrote matthew: Matthew (Levi), the tax collector turned apostle, wrote Matthew (~50-70 AD). Written to a Jewish audience, presenting Jesus as the promised Messiah and King.
who wrote mark: John Mark wrote Mark (~55-65 AD), based on Peter's preaching. The shortest Gospel, emphasizing Jesus as the suffering Servant and Son of God.
who wrote luke: Luke, a physician and companion of Paul, wrote Luke (~60 AD). The most detailed Gospel, written for Theophilus, emphasizing Jesus as Savior of all people.
who wrote john: John, the beloved disciple, wrote John (~85-95 AD). The most theological Gospel, emphasizing Jesus as the Word (Logos), the Son of God, and the way to eternal life (John 3:16, 14:6).
who wrote acts: Luke wrote Acts (~62 AD), the sequel to his Gospel. It records the birth of the Church at Pentecost, the spread of the Gospel through Peter and Paul, and ends with Paul under house arrest in Rome.
who wrote romans: The Apostle Paul wrote Romans (~57 AD), with Tertius as his amanuensis (Rom 16:22). It is his theological masterpiece — justification by faith, the righteousness of God, and life in the Spirit.
who wrote 1 corinthians: Paul wrote 1 Corinthians (~55 AD) from Ephesus. He addresses divisions, immorality, spiritual gifts, the resurrection, and love (1 Cor 13).
who wrote 2 corinthians: Paul wrote 2 Corinthians (~55-56 AD) from Macedonia. A deeply personal letter defending his apostleship and describing the new covenant ministry.
who wrote galatians: Paul wrote Galatians (~48-55 AD). Theme: justification by faith alone, not by works of the law. Key verse: 'I have been crucified with Christ' (Gal 2:20).
who wrote ephesians: Paul wrote Ephesians (~60-62 AD) from prison. Theme: the unity of the Church in Christ, the mystery of the Gospel, and the armor of God (Eph 6).
who wrote philippians: Paul wrote Philippians (~62 AD) from prison. Theme: joy in Christ despite circumstances. Key verse: 'I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me' (Phil 4:13).
who wrote colossians: Paul wrote Colossians (~60-62 AD) from prison. Theme: the supremacy and sufficiency of Christ over all spiritual powers (Col 1:15-20).
who wrote 1 thessalonians: Paul wrote 1 Thessalonians (~51 AD), likely his earliest letter. Themes: the second coming of Christ, holy living, and the rapture (1 Thess 4:13-18).
who wrote 2 thessalonians: Paul wrote 2 Thessalonians (~52 AD), clarifying the day of the Lord and correcting misunderstandings about Christ's return.
who wrote 1 timothy: Paul wrote 1 Timothy (~62-64 AD) to his protégé Timothy at Ephesus. Instructions on church leadership, false teaching, and godliness.
who wrote 2 timothy: Paul wrote 2 Timothy (~66-67 AD), his final letter before martyrdom. Theme: guard the Gospel, endure hardship, preach the word (2 Tim 4:2).
who wrote titus: Paul wrote Titus (~62-64 AD) to Titus on Crete. Instructions on church order and good works as evidence of grace.
who wrote philemon: Paul wrote Philemon (~60-62 AD) from prison, appealing for the runaway slave Onesimus — a picture of reconciliation in Christ.
who wrote hebrews: Author unknown. Candidates include Paul, Apollos, Barnabas, or Priscilla. Written to Jewish Christians (~60-70 AD), presenting Christ as superior to all — prophets, angels, Moses, the priesthood, the covenant. Key verse: 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever' (Heb 13:8).
who wrote james: James, the half-brother of Jesus and leader of the Jerusalem church, wrote James (~45-50 AD). Theme: faith without works is dead (James 2:26). Likely the earliest NT letter.
who wrote 1 peter: Peter wrote 1 Peter (~62-64 AD) from Rome ('Babylon'). Theme: suffering for Christ with hope, holy living, and the living stone.
who wrote 2 peter: Peter wrote 2 Peter (~65-68 AD), his final letter before martyrdom. Theme: beware false teachers, grow in grace, and look for the day of the Lord.
who wrote 1 john: John the apostle wrote 1 John (~85-95 AD). Theme: God is love, and love is proven by obedience (1 John 4:8, 5:3). Written against Gnostic errors.
who wrote 2 john: John wrote 2 John (~85-95 AD), a brief letter urging love and warning against deceivers who deny Christ's incarnation.
who wrote 3 john: John wrote 3 John (~85-95 AD), commending Gaius for hospitality and warning against Diotrephes' pride.
who wrote jude: Jude, the half-brother of Jesus, wrote Jude (~65-80 AD). Theme: contend for the faith against false teachers who pervert grace into licentiousness.
who wrote revelation: John the apostle wrote Revelation (~95 AD) while exiled on Patmos. It is the only NT prophecy book, revealing Christ's triumph, the judgments, and the new heaven and earth (Rev 21-22).
how many books in the bible: 66 books total — 39 in the Old Testament and 27 in the New Testament. Written by ~40 authors over ~1,500 years (c. 1400 BC - c. 100 AD), yet with one unified message: God's redemption through Christ.
how many books in the old testament: 39 books in the Old Testament: 5 Law (Pentateuch), 12 History, 5 Poetry/Wisdom, 5 Major Prophets, 12 Minor Prophets.
how many books in the new testament: 27 books in the New Testament: 4 Gospels, 1 History (Acts), 13 Pauline Epistles, 8 General Epistles, 1 Prophecy (Revelation).
what is the longest book in the bible: Psalms is the longest book in the Bible by chapter count (150 chapters) and word count. Jeremiah is the longest prophetic book.
what is the shortest book in the bible: 3 John is the shortest book in the Bible by word count. Obadiah is the shortest Old Testament book (21 verses).
what is the longest chapter in the bible: Psalm 119 is the longest chapter in the Bible (176 verses), an acrostic poem devoted to the Word of God.
what is the shortest chapter in the bibl: Psalm 117 is the shortest chapter in the Bible (2 verses): 'Praise the Lord, all nations! Extol him, all peoples!'
what is the shortest verse in the bible: 'Jesus wept' (John 11:35) is the shortest verse in the Bible, showing Christ's compassion at Lazarus' tomb.
when was the bible written: The Bible was written over approximately 1,500 years (c. 1400 BC to c. 100 AD) by ~40 authors on three continents (Asia, Africa, Europe) in three languages (Hebrew, Aramaic, Greek).
what language was the bible written in: The Old Testament was written primarily in Hebrew (with some Aramaic in Daniel and Ezra). The New Testament was written in Greek (Koine).
who translated the bible into english: William Tyndale (d. 1536) was the first to translate the Bible into English from the original languages. The King James Version (1611) drew heavily on Tyndale's work and became the most influential English Bible.
what is the kjv: The King James Version (KJV) is an English translation of the Bible commissioned by King James I, published in 1611. Translated by 47 scholars from the Textus Receptus (NT) and Masoretic Text (OT), it has shaped English language and culture for over 400 years.
